t: A rectangle is 4 squares long and 3 squares wide. Tom says the area is 7 square units. What did Tom do wrong?
o: He added 4 + 3 instead of counting all the squares
e: Tom added 4 + 3 = 7. But the area is all the squares: 3 rows of 4 = 12 square units. He should count all squares, not add the sides!

t: A rug covers 8 square units. A mat covers 5 square units. You put them side by side with no overlap. What is the total area?
e: 8 + 5 = 13 square units total. When shapes do not overlap, you add their areas!

t: Sara says a rectangle that is 5 squares long and 2 squares wide has an area of 52 square units. What did she do wrong?
o: She wrote the numbers next to each other instead of counting — the area is 10
e: Sara wrote 5 and 2 together as '52.' The real area is 2 rows of 5 = 10 square units!

t: A garden is 6 squares long and 3 squares wide. A shed covers 4 of those squares. How many uncovered squares are left?
e: Garden area: 3 rows of 6 = 18 squares. Then 18 - 4 = 14 uncovered squares.

t: Two rectangles each have an area of 8 square units. Can they have different shapes?
o: No, they must look the same
Yes — one could be 4x2 and another 8x1
Yes, but only if they are circles
e: A 4-by-2 rectangle and an 8-by-1 rectangle both have 8 square units but look very different!

t: A rectangle is 5 squares long and 4 squares wide. Jake says the area is 9 square units. What did Jake do wrong?
o: He added 5 + 4 instead of counting all the squares
e: Jake added 5 + 4 = 9. But the real area is 4 rows of 5 = 20 square units. He should count all the squares!

t: A poster is 6 squares long and 2 squares wide. You cut off a piece that is 4 square units. How many square units are left?
e: Poster area: 2 rows of 6 = 12 square units. 12 - 4 = 8 square units left!

t: Shape A is 3 by 4 and Shape B is 2 by 7. Which has a bigger area?
e: Shape A: 3 rows of 4 = 12. Shape B: 2 rows of 7 = 14. Shape B is bigger by 2 square units!

t: Maria covers a rectangle with 15 square tiles. She says the rectangle could be 5 squares long and 3 squares wide. Is she right?
e: 3 rows of 5 squares = 15 squares total. Maria is correct!

t: A floor is 5 squares long and 5 squares wide. A table covers 6 of those squares. How many floor squares can you still see?
e: Floor area: 5 rows of 5 = 25. Then 25 - 6 = 19 squares you can still see!

t: Ben says a 3-by-3 square and a 1-by-9 rectangle have the same area. Is he correct?
e: 3 x 3 = 9 and 1 x 9 = 9. Both have 9 square units! Same area, different shapes.

t: A rectangle is 6 squares long and 3 squares wide. Lily says the area is 9. What did she do wrong?
o: She added 6 + 3 instead of counting all squares
e: Lily got 9 by only counting 3 x 3 = one side. The full area is 3 rows of 6 = 18 square units!

t: A wall needs 24 square tiles. You have placed 17 tiles so far. How many more do you need?
e: 24 - 17 = 7 more tiles needed!

t: Shape A is 4 by 4. Shape B is 3 by 5. How many more square units does Shape A have?
e: Shape A: 4 x 4 = 16. Shape B: 3 x 5 = 15. Shape A has 1 more square unit!

t: A rectangle has an area of 12 square units. It is 4 squares long. How many squares wide is it?
e: If 4 rows make 12 total, each row has 12 / 4 = 3 squares. It is 3 squares wide!

t: A playground has two sections. Section A is 4 by 3 and Section B is 2 by 5. What is the total area?
e: Section A: 4 x 3 = 12. Section B: 2 x 5 = 10. Total: 12 + 10 = 22 square units!

t: Noah says a rectangle that is 2 by 6 has a bigger area than a 3 by 4 rectangle. Is he right?
o: No, they have the same area (12 square units)
e: 2 x 6 = 12 and 3 x 4 = 12. They have the same area! Different shapes, equal areas.

t: A rectangle has an area of 20 square units. It is 5 squares long. How many squares wide is it?
e: If 5 squares long and area is 20, then width = 20 / 5 = 4 squares wide!

t: Ava tiled a 3-by-6 rectangle but miscounted and said the area is 36. What did she likely do?
o: She wrote 3 and 6 next to each other as 36
e: Ava put 3 and 6 together to get 36. The real area is 3 rows of 6 = 18 square units!

t: A patio is 4 by 5. You add a 2-by-5 section next to it. What is the new total area?
e: Patio: 4 x 5 = 20. New section: 2 x 5 = 10. Total: 20 + 10 = 30 square units!
